name: Chandrahasa
description: Chandrahasa, meaning 'the laughter of the moon' or 'having blades as beautiful as the moon', is a legendary sword of immense power in Hindu mythology. It is most famously associated with Ravana, the ten-headed demon king of Lanka, although its origins predate him. The sword was originally given to Ravana by Lord Shiva as a boon for his intense devotion and penance. Chandrahasa is not merely a weapon but a divine artifact imbued with Shiva's energy, making it incredibly potent and capable of cleaving through virtually anything. Its wielder gains immense strength, protection, and the ability to overcome almost any obstacle. However, the sword also carries a curse: if wielded unjustly or for selfish purposes, it will ultimately lead to the wielder's downfall. While Ravana initially used Chandrahasa to conquer and protect his kingdom, his arrogance and eventual misuse of the sword's power contributed to his demise at the hands of Lord Rama. The sword represents the delicate balance between power and responsibility, and the consequences of unchecked ambition. It symbolizes the divine grace bestowed upon the worthy, but also the inevitable retribution for those who abuse it. The blade is described as being exceptionally sharp and radiant, capable of emitting its own light. Its hilt is often depicted as being adorned with precious gems and intricate carvings, reflecting its divine origin and the immense power it holds. The legend of Chandrahasa serves as a cautionary tale about the seductive nature of power and the importance of righteousness in its application.
subtitle: The Sword of Ravana, Gift of Shiva
origin: Gifted by Lord Shiva to Ravana

keyMyths.title: Ravana's Penance
keyMyths.description: Ravana performed intense penance to Lord Shiva, offering his heads as sacrifice. Pleased with his devotion, Shiva gifted him Chandrahasa.

keyMyths.title: The Curse of Chandrahasa
keyMyths.description: The sword is said to bring destruction if used for unjust purposes. Ravana's misuse of the sword ultimately led to his defeat by Rama.

sources.author: Valmiki
sources.description: The epic Ramayana details Ravana's possession and use of Chandrahasa.
sources.text: The epic Ramayana details Ravana's possession and use of Chandrahasa.

sources.author: Tulsidas
sources.description: A retelling of the Ramayana, also featuring Chandrahasa.
